questions.question: Explain the difference between grep, egrep, and fgrep with respect to regular expressions supported.
keyPoints: grep: basic regular expressions (BRE) - metacharacters like ., *, ^, $ are literal unless escaped
egrep: extended regular expressions (ERE) - supports +, ?, |, (), {} as metacharacters
fgrep: fixed strings - treats all characters as literals, no regex processing
egrep is equivalent to grep -E

questions.question: Write an egrep pattern to match valid phone numbers in the format XXX-XXX-XXXX where X is a digit.
keyPoints: Or equivalently: [[:digit:]]{3}-[[:digit:]]{3}-[[:digit:]]{4}
[0-9] or [[:digit:]] matches any digit
{3} and {4} are quantifiers specifying exact repetitions
The hyphens are literal characters matched as shown

questions.question: What are POSIX character classes? List at least four with their meanings.
keyPoints: [[:alpha:]] - matches any alphabetic character (a-z, A-Z)
[[:digit:]] - matches any digit (0-9)
[[:alnum:]] - matches any alphanumeric character (letters and digits)
[[:space:]] - matches any whitespace character (space, tab, newline)
[[:upper:]] - matches uppercase letters
[[:lower:]] - matches lowercase letters
[[:punct:]] - matches punctuation characters

questions.question: Explain the concept of backreferences in egrep with an example.
keyPoints: Backreferences allow referencing a previously matched group
Syntax: \1, \2, etc. refer to 1st, 2nd captured group
Example: (.)\1 matches consecutive identical characters like 'aa', 'bb'
Example: ([a-z])\1{2} matches three consecutive same letters like 'aaa'
Useful for finding repeated words or patterns in text

questions.question: Write egrep commands to: (a) Count lines containing 'error', (b) Show line numbers of matches, (c) Search case-insensitively, (d) Match whole words only.
keyPoints: (a) egrep -c 'error' filename
(b) egrep -n 'error' filename
(c) egrep -i 'error' filename
(d) egrep -w 'error' filename
These options can be combined: egrep -inw 'error' filename

questions.question: What is the difference between character class [abc] and grouping (abc)?
keyPoints: [abc] - character class: matches any ONE character from a, b, or c
(abc) - grouping: treats abc as a single unit for applying quantifiers/anchors
Example: (ab)+ matches 'ab', 'abab', 'ababab'
Example: [ab]+ matches 'a', 'b', 'ab', 'ba', 'aab', any combination
Grouping enables backreferences: (\w)\1 captures and reuses matched content

questions.question: Explain word boundary anchors \b with an example showing why egrep -w is different from plain pattern matching.
keyPoints: \b matches position between a word character and non-word character
Example: \bhello\b matches 'hello' but not 'helloo' or 'ohello'
egrep -w 'hello' achieves the same result automatically
Without -w or \b: 'hello' in pattern matches 'hello' within larger words
Useful for exact word searches in text processing

questions.question: Design an egrep pattern to validate email addresses. The pattern should match basic email format (local-part@domain.tld). Explain each component of your pattern.
keyPoints: ^ - anchors to start of line
[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+ - local part: letters, digits, special chars, one or more
@ - literal @ symbol
[A-Za-z0-9.-]+ - domain name: alphanumeric and dots/hyphens
\. - literal dot (escaped)
[A-Za-z]{2,} - TLD: at least 2 letters
$ - anchors to end of line
This is a basic validation - real email validation is more complex

questions.question: Write a comprehensive note on extended regular expression metacharacters supported by egrep. Provide examples for each metacharacter.
keyPoints: + : one or more - 'a+' matches a, aa, aaa
? : zero or one - 'colou?r' matches color, colour
| : alternation - 'cat|dog' matches cat or dog
( ) : grouping - '(ab)+' groups and repeats
{n} : exactly n times - 'a{3}' matches aaa
{n,} : at least n times - 'a{2,}' matches aa, aaa, aaaa
{n,m} : between n and m - 'a{2,4}' matches aa, aaaa, aaaaa
. : any character except newline
^ : start of line anchor
$ : end of line anchor
[ ] : character class with negation [^]
\ : escape special characters
Examples demonstrate each metacharacter's usage

questions.question: Explain the following egrep options with examples: -i, -v, -c, -n, -l, -r, -w, -o
keyPoints: -i : case insensitive - egrep -i 'hello' matches Hello, HELLO
-v : invert match - egrep -v 'error' shows lines NOT containing error
-c : count matches - egrep -c 'pattern' prints number of matching lines
-n : show line numbers - egrep -n 'pattern' prefixes line numbers
-l : show filenames only - egrep -l 'pattern' lists files with matches
-r : recursive search - egrep -r 'pattern' searches in directories
-w : whole word - egrep -w 'hello' matches whole words only
-o : only matching - egrep -o 'pattern' shows only matched portions
Examples for each option demonstrating practical usage

questions.question: Create an egrep pattern to extract all URLs from a text file. The URLs may start with http://, https://, or www. and contain alphanumeric characters, dots, slashes, and hyphens.
keyPoints: https? matches http or https (s is optional)
(http://|https://|www\.) matches any of the three URL prefixes
[a-zA-Z0-9.-]+ matches domain name characters
(?: ... ) is non-capturing group for optional path
[a-zA-Z0-9./_-]* matches path characters after domain
This pattern handles most common URL formats
Explain each component in detail
